title: Lecture 4 fundamentals of options and option strategies
body:
Lecture 4 – Fundamentals of Options and Option Strategies

Hull Chapter 9 & 10 & 11  Terminology and notation

• An option is an instrument that gives the right, but not the obligation, to trade the underlying asset at a fixed price • Options that give the right to buy the underlying are known as call options • Options that give the right to sell the underlying are known as put options

• Like a futures contract, an option has: • A fixed price at which the underlying will be bought (for calls) or sold (for puts), known as the strike price, K • A date of expiration, known as the expiration date, T • An underlying asset, S

Terminology and notation

• The holder of an option has a long position, pays the premium and chooses when to exercise.

• The issuer of an option has a short position, receives the premium but sells the right to exercise.

• Options that can only be exercised at a specific time-of-maturity, T, are called European options

• Options that can be exercised at any time prior to T are called American options

• We’ll denote the prices of options as follows: • c (European call option) • p (European put option) • C (American call option) • P (American put option) 3  Options – markets and underlying assets

• Common underlying assets for options are • Stocks (typically American options) • Stock indices (typically European options) • Foreign currencies • Futures contracts (including futures written on commodities)

• Trading options is similar to trading futures • Actual markets have bid-ask spreads, • Each trade incurred a fixed cost (commission) • If you write a naked option, you must post and maintain a margin • The option is naked if you don’t hold the underlying asset • Order types are the same as for futures and stocks • Market orders, limit orders, stop-loss orders, etc.

Payoffs

• At time T the holder of a call option will either exercise it (if 𝑆𝑇 > 𝐾) or throw it away (if 𝐾 > 𝑆𝑇 ) • The (European) call option payoff is max(𝑆𝑇 – 𝐾; 0) • The opposite is true for a put option • The (European) put option payoff 𝑖𝑠 max(𝐾 – 𝑆𝑇 ; 0)

Option market conventions and terminology

• Options are traded on exchanges or OTC • In the U.S., stock options are American, while index options are European • U.S. contracts expire on the third Friday of each month • A January call trading on IBM is a call option on IBM with an expiration date in January. The precise expiration date is the Saturday immediately following the third Friday of the expiration month. The last day on which options trade is the third Friday of the expiration month. • Strike prices are typically traded at intervals of $2.5, $5 or $25 (depending on the price of the underlying asset) • The payoff of an option if it expired today is called the intrinsic value of the option • If the intrinsic value is positive the option is said to be in-the-money • If the spot price is equal to the strike price the option is said to be at-the-money • Other options are said to be out-of-the-money

Mechanical changes in the stock price

• Some events lead to very predictable (almost mechanical) changes in the stock price • The terms of option contracts are adjusted for most such events other than regular cash dividends • The terms are adjusted for stock splits • If a company is worth $100 and has 50 stocks outstanding, each stock is worth $2 • If the company does a 2:1 stock split, there will be twice as many stocks (100) and each will be worth half of what they were worth before ($1) • When that happens, options are also “split”, i.e. the numbers double and the strike price halves • Stock dividends work in almost exactly the same way and are also adjusted for • If a firm pays out $1 per share to their shareholders as a cash dividend • The share price drops by $1 on the ex-dividend date • The terms of the option contract is not adjusted for this predictable effect • Special dividends, however, will lead to adjustments at the discretion of the exchange • Typically for those that are more than 10% of the stock price  Factors affecting the value of an option

• Moneyness (always a positive effect) • Options that are deep-in-the-money are more likely to pay off • Cash dividends (negative for calls and positive for puts) • Since dividends predictably decrease the stock price, they will affect the moneyness • Underlying volatility (always positive) • The greater the underlying volatility, the greater the chance that the moneyness will increase • Interest rates (positive for calls and negative for puts) • Expiration time (typically positive) • The longer the time-to-expiration, the greater the chance that the moneyness will increase • If we increase the expiration time to include an additional dividend, the dividend effect may dominate • If the volatility is low and the interest rate is high, the discounting effect could in principle dominate for puts  Upper bounds on option prices

• American options are like European option but with more rights, so it must be that • 𝑐𝑡 ≤ 𝐶𝑡 • 𝑝𝑡 ≤ 𝑃𝑡 • In the best case scenario, call options are exercised and converted into a stock (or whatever the underlying asset is), so: • 𝑐𝑡 ≤ 𝑆𝑡 • 𝐶𝑡 ≤ 𝑆𝑡 • If these relationships were not true, an arbitrageur could easily make a riskless profit by buying the stock and selling the call option. • In the best case scenario, put options are exercised and converted into $K, so: • 𝑃𝑡 ≤ 𝐾 • 𝑝𝑡 ≤ 𝐾𝑒 −𝑟(𝑇−𝑡) (we get the money at time T) • If this were not true, an arbitrageur could make a riskless profit by writing the option and investing the proceeds of the sale at the risk-free interest rate. 9  Option strategies

• By combining different instruments, we can achieve many new pay-off functions • pay-off diagrams display the cash-flows at the exercise date • profit diagrams also take the initial cost of setting up the strategy into account • pay-off diagrams should not be confused with present values

• For ease of discussion, we focus mostly on European options here • This makes it easier to focus on time T payoff • Trying to implement the strategy using American options may have very different consequences…

• Also, we will ignore dividends for now

Building blocks

• We consider four building blocks in our strategies

• We can take either long or short positions in any component

Characteristics of a strategy

•Whenever we set up a strategy we should think of the following:

• What are the components of the strategy? • Often there are multiple ways to construct the same payoff function

• What is the cash-flow when setting up the strategy, e.g., the cost?

• What are the risks of setting up the strategy? • Are the losses limited or illimited?

• What purpose would the strategy achieve? • Hedge a certain risk exposure? • Bet on certain beliefs?

The covered call

•Components • One stock • One short call •Purpose: Gain the call premium •Downside: Loss of upside from the stock

An alternative construction of a covered call

•Consider a portfolio consisting of one bond with face value K and one short put option (written on that stock) with a strike price of K

Put-call parity

•The covered call and its replication are worth exactly the same at time T regardless of 𝑆𝑇

•In the absence of arbitrage, they must also be worth the same at any time 𝑡 < 𝑇 −𝑟(𝑇−𝑡) 𝑆𝑡 – 𝑐𝑡 = 𝐾𝑒 − 𝑝𝑡

•This no-arbitrage relationship is called put-call parity • Like other no-arbitrage relationships it holds in reality to a very close approximation

Lower bounds on European option prices (no dividends)

•From put-call parity we have that

• Since neither the call or the put option can be worth less than zero (we could always choose not to exercise), lower bounds are given by:

Option spreads

•Components: Several options of the same kind (but typically with different strike prices)

•Purpose: Speculate on certain stock price movements

•Risks: Limited to the upfront cost

The bull spread

• Components: • One long call option with strike price 𝐾1 • One short call option with strike price 𝐾2 > 𝐾1 • Considerations: • Choose distance between 𝐾1 and 𝐾2 • Greater distance gives higher payoff (𝐾2 − 𝐾1 ) and, • Higher cost (pay higher in long when 𝐾1 lower and receive lower in short when 𝐾2 is higher) • Choose relationship between K and 𝑆𝑡 • If 𝐾 – 𝑆𝑡 is large setup costs are lower, but so is the probability of a payoff (less likely to exercise)

The bull spread – an alternative construction

• We could achieve a similar payoff using put options • One long put with strike price 𝐾1 • One short put with strike price 𝐾2 • The payoff function has the same shape but is lowered by (𝐾2 − 𝐾1 ) • We could make the payoff function exactly the same by adding a bond with a face value of (𝐾2 − 𝐾1 ) at expiration.

The bull spread – an alternative construction

• Consider the two portfolios in light of put-call parity

• Our first construction would have a price of 𝑐1 – 𝑐2 (where the sub-index denotes the strike price)

• By put-call parity:

−𝑟 𝑇−𝑡 −𝑟 𝑇−𝑡 𝑐1 − 𝑐2 = 𝑝1 + 𝑆𝑡 − 𝐾1 𝑒 − 𝑝2 − 𝑆𝑡 + 𝐾2 𝑒 =𝑝1 − 𝑝2 − 𝐾1 𝑒 −𝑟 𝑇−𝑡 + 𝐾2 𝑒 −𝑟 𝑇−𝑡

• We can often apply put-call parity to find alternative ways of constructing a certain pay-off function

The bear spread

• Suppose that we take short the last position (ignoring the bond) • We are now hoping for the opposite outcome, i.e., a falling stock price

• This is known as a bear spread

• Components: • One long put option with strike price 𝐾2 • One short put option with strike price 𝐾1

The bear spread

• A combination of a bear spread and a bull spread is known as a box spread

• Since the two spreads “cancel each other out”, the payoff is always 𝐾2 − 𝐾1

• We can see this either graphically or via put-call parity (try that out at home)

The butterfly spread

• Components • One long call with strike price 𝐾1 • Two short calls with strike price 𝐾2 > 𝐾1 • One long call with strike price 𝐾3 > 𝐾2

• Purpose: Bet on low volatility

• Downside: The setup cost is positive

• We typically pick 𝐾2 close to 𝑆𝑡

• The distance 𝐾3 − 𝐾1 affects: • The “winning interval” • The cost of setting up the strategy

• By shorting the strategy, we can bet on high volatility 23  Calendar spreads

• Components: A calendar spread consists of a (long/short) pair of options with the same strike price but different expiration dates • The short option has the early expiration date and the long option has the late expiration date • The choice of option type is not very important for the payoff • We typically consider the payoff at the first expiration date • If the short option ends up just out of the money it is worthless, whereas the long option still has a decent chance of ending up in-the-money • At that point the payoff is maximized • If the short option ends up deep out-of-the-money it is again worthless, but the long option is also likely to end up out-of-the-money • At that point the payoff is close to zero • If the short (call) option ends up deep in-the-money it is costs the issuer 𝑆𝑇 − 𝐾 and the long option is very likely to end up in the money as well, and will be worth close to 𝑆𝑇 − 𝐾 • At that point the payoff is close to zero again

Straddles

• Components: • One long call option and one long put option • Purpose: • Bet on high volatility (straddles are similar to short butterfly spreads in that sense) • Downside: • Setup costs are positive • If we set 𝐾 = 𝑆𝑡 , the straddle pays off for any movement in the spot price • If we think price increases are more likely we can add another call • This is known as a strap • If we think price decreases are more likely we can add another put • This is known as a strip

Exercise questions

• Hull chapter 9: 8, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 19

• Hull chapter 10: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17

• Hull chapter 11: 2, 3, 10, 11
